title: Master Summary: English Tenses
content:
### Present Simple Tense
Used for permanent situations, general truths, and habits. Structure: Subject + V.1 (s/es with he/she/it). Key markers: always, usually, often, every day.

---

### Present Continuous Tense
Used for actions happening exactly now or around the present time. Structure: Subject + is/am/are + V-ing. Key markers: now, at the moment, currently.

---

### Present Perfect Tense
Links past actions to the present. Used for experiences or actions with current relevance. Structure: Subject + have/has + V.3. Key markers: since, for, already, yet, just.

---

### Past Simple Tense
Used for completed actions at a specific time in the past. Structure: Subject + V.2 (regular -ed or irregular). Key markers: yesterday, ago, last week.

---

### Past Continuous Tense
Used for actions that were in progress at a specific time in the past. Structure: Subject + was/were + V-ing. Often used with 'while' or 'when'.

---

### Past Perfect Tense
The 'past of the past'. Used for an action that happened before another action in the past. Structure: Subject + had + V.3.

---

### Future Simple Tense
Used for spontaneous decisions, predictions without evidence, or promises. Structure: Subject + will + V.inf. Alternative: 'be going to' for planned actions.

---

### Future Continuous Tense
Used for actions that will be in progress at a specific time in the future. Structure: Subject + will + be + V-ing.

---

### Future Perfect Tense
Used for an action that will be completed by a certain time in the future. Structure: Subject + will + have + V.3. Often uses 'by...'.

---

### Passive Voice in All Tenses
Transformation rules from active to passive. Remember to keep the tense of 'be' consistent with the original sentence.

title: Master Summary: grammar
content:
### Present Perfect Continuous
Emphasizes the duration of an action that started in the past and continues now. Structure: Subject + have/has + been + V-ing.

---

### Past Perfect Continuous
Focuses on the duration of an action that was happening up until another point in the past. Structure: Subject + had + been + V-ing.

---

### Future Perfect Continuous
Used to show how long an action will have been in progress at a point in the future. Structure: Subject + will + have + been + V-ing.

---

### Passive Voice Basics
The focus is on the object rather than the doer. Structure: Object + be + V.3. Example: 'The cake was eaten by Mary.'

---

### Zero Conditional
Used for general truths and scientific facts. Structure: If + Present Simple, Present Simple. Example: 'If you heat ice, it melts.'

---

### First Conditional
Used for real or possible future situations. Structure: If + Present Simple, will + V.inf. Example: 'If it rains, I will stay home.'

---

### Second Conditional
Used for hypothetical or imaginary present/future situations. Structure: If + Past Simple, would + V.inf. Example: 'If I were you, I would go.'

---

### Third Conditional
Used for imaginary past situations (regrets). Structure: If + Past Perfect, would + have + V.3. Example: 'If I had studied, I would have passed.'

---

### Mixed Conditionals
Combines elements of second and third conditionals to show past causes with present results or vice versa. Example: 'If I had won the lottery (past), I would be rich now (present).'

---

### Error ID: Subject-Verb Agreement
Singular subjects take singular verbs; plural subjects take plural verbs. Watch out for phrases between subject and verb.

---

### Error ID: Parallel Structure
Items in a list or series must have the same grammatical form. Example: 'I like swimming, hiking, and to run' is incorrect.

---

### Error ID: Pronoun Case
Ensure pronouns are used in the correct case (subjective vs objective). Example: 'Between you and me' (not 'you and I').

---

### Error ID: Misplaced Modifiers
Modifiers must be placed next to the word they describe. Example: 'Walking down the street, the tree fell' implies the tree was walking.

---

### Grammar: Inversion for Emphasis
Inverting the subject and verb after certain negative adverbs (Never, Seldom, Rarely). Example: 'Never have I seen such beauty.'

---

### The Subjunctive Mood
Used for wishes, suggestions, or hypothetical situations. Examples: 'I suggest that he be on time' or 'If I were you.'

---

### Defining vs. Non-Defining Clauses
Defining clauses provide essential info (no commas). Non-defining provide extra info (with commas). Example: 'The man who lives next door is a doctor.'

---

### Error ID: Common Diction Errors
Confusing similar words: 'Affect' (verb) vs 'Effect' (noun), 'Complement' vs 'Compliment', 'Stationary' vs 'Stationery'.

---

### Articles with Abstract Nouns
General abstract nouns take no article ('Love is blind'). Specific instances take 'the' ('The love he felt was deep').

title: Master Summary: vocabulary
content:
### Vocabulary Roots: Spect, Dict, Scrib
Latin roots: 'Spect' (to look, e.g., inspect), 'Dict' (to say, e.g., predict), 'Scrib/Scrip' (to write, e.g., describe).

---

### Greek Roots: Bio, Geo, Tele
Greek roots: 'Bio' (life, e.g., biology), 'Geo' (earth, e.g., geography), 'Tele' (far, e.g., telephone).

---

### Common Prefixes: Un, Re, Dis
Prefixes change word meaning: 'Un-' (not, e.g., unhappy), 'Re-' (again, e.g., redo), 'Dis-' (opposite, e.g., disagree).

---

### Common Suffixes: Tion, Ness, Ly
Suffixes change word class: '-Tion' (noun, e.g., action), '-Ness' (noun, e.g., kindness), '-Ly' (adverb, e.g., slowly).

---

### Collocations: Verb + Noun
Words that naturally go together. 'Make a decision' (not 'do a decision'), 'Perform an experiment', 'Conduct research'.

---

### Phrasal Verbs in Academic Context
Replacing phrasal verbs with formal equivalents: 'Look into' -> 'Investigate', 'Find out' -> 'Discover', 'Put off' -> 'Postpone'.

---

### Denotation and Connotation
Denotation is the literal meaning. Connotation is the emotional/cultural association (e.g., 'Youthful' vs 'Childish').

title: Master Summary: reading
content:
### Reading: Skimming and Scanning
Skimming: Reading quickly for the main idea. Scanning: Searching for specific information like names or dates.

---

### Reading: Context Clues
Using surrounding words to guess the meaning of unknown vocabulary. Look for synonyms, antonyms, or examples.

---

### Reading: Inference & Tone
Reading between the lines. Identifying the author's attitude (positive, negative, neutral) based on word choice.

title: Master Summary: writing
content:
### Academic Hedging
Using cautious language in research. Instead of 'This proves', use 'This suggests' or 'It is possible that'.

---

### Academic Reporting Verbs
Choosing precise verbs to introduce sources: 'Argues', 'Claims', 'Suggests', 'Demonstrates', 'Notes', 'Challenges'.

---

### Nominalization
Converting verbs/adjectives into nouns to sound more academic. 'We analyzed' -> 'The analysis was conducted'.

---

### Cohesive Devices: Addition & Contrast
Addition: 'Furthermore', 'Moreover'. Contrast: 'However', 'Nevertheless', 'Conversely', 'In contrast'.
